repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
**Problem**: During initial "bootstrap" via lockfile synchronization, the whole plugin specification is reconstructed from the lockfile data, ignoring potential user changes added in the first `vim.pack.add()`. This is enough in most situations since it is the only data needed for actual installation.

However, this affects specification passed to `PackChanged[Pre]` events. In particular, `data` field is missing which can be a problem if there is a `PackChanged kind=install` hook that uses that field (like with some kind of `build` method used during install). And there might be different `version` set in `vim.pack.add()`.

**Solution**: Pass the `specs` input of the first `vim.pack.add()` down to lockfile synchronization and use it to reconstruct plugin specification for the to-be-installed plugin. If present among the user's `specs`, it is used but with forced `src` from the lockfile (as it is the one used during installation).

Note that this still has a caveat when using separate `vim.pack.add()`, as only the specs from the first input (when the lockfile synchronization happens) is taken into account.

---

Resolve #38125

comment_regular_data.body:
> Note that this still has a caveat when using separate vim.pack.add(), as only the specs from the first input (when the lockfile synchronization happens) is taken into account.

This is what [gets me](https://www.reddit.com/r/neovim/comments/1q41n6q/comment/o9n9fpd/?utm_source=share&utm_medium=web3x&utm_name=web3xcss&utm_term=1&utm_content=share_button), as I use separate `vim.pack.add()` statements so that I can have my initialization/configuration code together with the installation. This behaviour is not obvious at all, and I've spent quite a bit of time debugging this with `debug.traceback()`.

I really wish lockfiles did not exist. I'm not sure what they bring me, the state that's in the repos is good enough for me. When I want to update/pull, I'll run update. Is there any way I can disable them? Even hard-removing the lockfile prior to startup does not seem to fix it for me. I think `vim.pack` may be regenerating the lockfile from on-disk state. 

`mini.deps`, worked a lot better for me in this regard.  But I also enjoy the idea of a lower-dependency setup, so I want to  switch to `vim.pack`.

> I really wish lockfiles did not exist. I'm not sure what they bring me, the state that's in the repos is good enough for me.

- It is used as a “latest working state” reference when bootstrapping the config on the new machine. In this case during the very first `vim.pack.add()` call all plugins that are present in the lockfile but absent on disk are installed all at once. This ensures that even lazy loaded plugins are installed right away.

- It contains information about plugin’s version, that is useful when updating if there are not-yet-active plugins (since there is no `vim.pack.add()` call yet to say which version it follows).

- It enables easier "revert the latest update" workflow by reverting the lockfile and `vim.pack.update(nil, { target = 'lockfile' })`.

- In future it will allow to store more information about plugins to perform more actions without much startup overhead. Like provide information about minimum expected Neovim version (per plugin) or plugin dependencies (to at least ensure that they are already loaded).

> Is there any way I can disable them? Even hard-removing the lockfile prior to startup does not seem to fix it for me. I think `vim.pack` may be regenerating the lockfile from on-disk state.

This might be a good candidate for yet another (`vim.pack.config()`) #34772 flag/feature. Maybe together with #36078 (like `lockfile = string|boolean` option).

But in the meantime everything works as expected.

> I really wish lockfiles did not exist. I'm not sure what they bring me, the state that's in the repos is good enough for me.

We definitely need a clear, compelling answer for this.

>  call all plugins that are present in the lockfile but absent on disk are installed all at once. **This ensures that even lazy loaded plugins are installed right away.**

Why? What are lazy plugins in this sense?

> Like provide information about **minimum expected Neovim version** (per plugin) or plugin dependencies (to at least ensure that they are already loaded).

Where does this information come from? Is this saved from a `vim.pack.add()` from a prior execution? Or is it some metadata in the plugin repo? (And if it is, why does it need to be in the lockfile?)

If it is from a prior `vim.pack.add()` statement, then you don't know whether the user (configurer) changed it in the meantime. Suppose I made a mistake, now the bad/unintended state is saved in the lockfile.

My configuration is my source of truth, and the lockfile is getting in between my truth and me.

The only thing of the things you mentioned I could entertain is:

> It contains information about plugin’s version, that is useful when updating if there are not-yet-active plugins (since there is no vim.pack.add() call yet to say which version it follows).

But couldn't this be solved a different way? I [update](https://github.com/aktau/dotfiles/blob/57f378d52b91d6b1cac98aa83f73a1814616d156/bin/aktau-upgrade-tools#L31) after rebuilding Neovim: 

```
$ nvim --headless -c 'lua ran = false ; vim.schedule(function() vim.pack.update(nil, { force = true }) ; ran = true end) ; vim.wait(1000, function() return ran end)' -c 'TSUpdateSync' -c 'qa'
```

According to `man nvim`, `-c` runs after processing the `.vimrc` (I assume this means `init.lua`), so the `vim.pack.add()` statements should have happened already.

The one caveat would be conditional plugin adds. But here I would prefer a different solution:

```
-- Previously:
if in_git_repo(vim.uv.cwd()) then
  -- Only activate fugitive in git repos:
  vim.pack.add({ src = "https://github.com/tpope/vim-fugitive" }, { confirm = true })
end

-- Proposed
vim.pack.add({ src = "https://github.com/tpope/vim-fugitive" }, { confirm = true, activate = in_git_repo(vim.uv.cwd()) })
```

> The only thing of the things you mentioned I could entertain is:
> 
> > It contains information about plugin’s version, that is useful when updating if there are not-yet-active plugins (since there is no vim.pack.add() call yet to say which version it follows).
> 
> But couldn't this be solved a different way?

That seems more like the responsibility of a "packspec". Though the "resolved" state is stored in a lockfile.

But this is a worthwhile discussion. Maybe lockfile is not something we need in 0.12 ...

> Why? What are lazy plugins in this sense?

Plugins that are `vim.pack.add()`ed not during startup. Like in `InsertEnter` callback.

> > Like provide information about **minimum expected Neovim version** (per plugin) or plugin dependencies (to at least ensure that they are already loaded).
> 
> Where does this information come from?

That point starts with "In future". The idea is for plugins to provide that information in `pkg.json` file (see #34778).

> My configuration is my source of truth, and the lockfile is getting in between my truth and me.
> 
> The only thing of the things you mentioned I could entertain is:

You probably missed the "It is used as a “latest working state” reference when bootstrapping the config on the new machine.". This is one of the main reasons for the lockfile: to be able to have the same plugin revisions installing on a new machine.

> But couldn't this be solved a different way?

By forcing users to have a single `vim.pack.add()` call that defines "the source of truth for all plugins". But that is already possible with a single `vim.pack.add()` and would make several `vim.pack.add()` not feasible.

---

> But this is a worthwhile discussion. Maybe lockfile is not something we need in 0.12 ...

@justinmk, yes, it is something needed in 0.12. It enables important workflows that are not possible without it (install or restore to the exact version), makes having lazy loaded plugins nicer, and opens the door for future useful features.

That was also the number 1 requested feature after the initial release (somewhat confirmed by positive reactions in #34776 and #35827).

> **That point starts with "In future". The idea is for plugins to provide that information in pkg.json file (see https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34778).**

Why couldn't it be read from the repository?

> > Why? What are lazy plugins in this sense?
>
> **Plugins that are vim.pack.add()ed not during startup. Like in InsertEnter callback.**

Couldn't that also be done (for those that need it) using something like:

```lua
vim.pack.add({ ... }, { activate = false }) -- Indicate that we want this (lazy) plugin to be installed, don't add it to rtp yet.
aucmd("InsertEnter", function(ev) vim.pack.add({ ... }) end)
```

> > But couldn't this be solved a different way?
>
> **By forcing users to have a single vim.pack.add() call that defines "the source of truth for all plugins". But that is already possible with a single vim.pack.add() and would make several vim.pack.add() not feasible.**

That is one way.  But has the downside of  needing a centralized place to declare all plugins. Especially with my work/personal/common splits, that's hard. Even if it weren't, that'd require me to split install from config like in the old days.

I (believe I) proposed another way, PTAL.

> > My configuration is my source of truth, and the lockfile is getting in between my truth and me.
>
> **You probably missed the "It is used as a “latest working state” reference when bootstrapping the config on the new machine.". This is one of the main reasons for the lockfile: to be able to have the same plugin revisions installing on a new machine.**

While I don't have much use of this (especially considering it pessimizes more frequent use cases like `update`), let's assume that this is common and important.

Why not use bootstrap information on the relevant `vim.pack.add()` call then? Right now it seems to initialize/install everything on the first `vim.pack.add()` call, causing these inconsistencies. If instead it looked up the locked version of $REPO on the `vim.pack.add({ src = "$REPO" })`, then it could at l east use the correct arguments.

I'd still prefer not to have a lockfile, but I'll take that over the current inconsistent behaviour.

> Why couldn't it be read from the repository?

Because checking plugin dependencies should be done on every startup and reading a file for every installed plugin is costly compared to reading a single lock file (that also helps in several other cases).

> Couldn't that also be done (for those that need it) using something like:

It could. The same way as the "absolutely needs `data` for the first install" can be solved in more than one way:
- The same dedicated `vim.pack.add()` at the top of the config.
- Make installation hook in `PackChanged` perform build not immediately if there is no `data` field. Like for example by setting a dedicated global flag that will be checked immediately after `vim.pack.add()` a target plugin. If it is set - perform the build.
- Force only a single `vim.pack.add()` on `VimEnter` after "collecting" all specifications from plugins into a dedicated global table. This emulates the "true single" `vim.pack.add()` approach but with specifications scattered across different places during startup.

> Why not use bootstrap information on the relevant `vim.pack.add()` call then? Right now it seems to initialize/install everything on the first `vim.pack.add()` call, causing these inconsistencies. If instead it looked up the locked version of $REPO on the `vim.pack.add({ src = "$REPO" })`, then it could at l east use the correct arguments.

I did think about this when implementing lockfile synchronization (#36338). There were several issues:

- Not doing lockfile synchronization (from which "install all plugins from the lockfile" is only one step) potentially makes lockfile and state on disk out of sync for the duration of startup. This might be problematic if the user decides to use `vim.pack.get()` or something during startup.
- With the current approach there is a visible performance gain when checking if the plugin needs to be installed. Scanning the directory with installed plugins once during the first `vim.pack.add()` *combined with lockfile sync* is faster than checking if the plugin is installed on every `vim.pack.add()`. This can be hacked around, of course, but that would require extra complexity. Which with the previous point seems to be too much.

> I'd still prefer not to have a lockfile, but I'll take that over the current inconsistent behaviour.

I suggested a solution to "not to have a lockfile" already, that I firmly believe is the right approach here: the `vim.pack.config()` with `lockfile` option.

The current way lockfile works is suitable for most configs in most situations. The edge case comes **only** during *first* install and if the user *chooses* to have complex config (custom builds in a `data` field), for which there are more than one workaround (see above) *and* for which there is a long term solution in the form of 'pkg.json' hooks. I think this is good enough, especially this close to the release.

A lot of words being spent here, which makes me think the use-case for the lockfile is not so clear cut. Sure, there is a use-case, but it's not a simple, clear story. Maybe it should be opt-in rather than opt-out. 

It seems to me that it currently gains a small perf improvement, at the cost of complicating the lifecycle and debugging steps.

> It seems to me that it currently gains a small perf improvement, at the cost of complicating the lifecycle and debugging steps.

@justinmk, please see [the list of reasons in this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38139#issuecomment-4030482055), there is not only "small performance improvement". I *assure you*, that lockfile being present brings more usability than it creates temporary pain points in some rare edge cases.

> Because checking plugin dependencies should be done on every startup and reading a file for every installed plugin is costly compared to reading a single lock file (that also helps in several other cases).

But it also invokes one of the hardest two problems in computer science: naming, caching, and off-by-one errors. This cache is causing the issues I'm seeing.

Apart from the lockfile, I assume `vim.pack.add` at least has to check for the existence of the plugin directory (if it didn't, it wouldn't know whether it needed to install on-demand).  If you believe that over time most plugins will have such a JSON file, you could instead directly read this JSON file, and fallback to just checking directory existence. 

A separate thought (though I don't know what fraction of time spent this currently is): instead of JSON decoding, one could use plain Lua (loadfile). It is (notably) [a configuration language](https://www.lua.org/pil/25.html) too.

Further, it may be possible to do a number of these fs operations concurrently using the libuv event loop. (Though of course concurrency is limited for separate `vim.pack.add` statements, but see below for a note on eager memoization)

> It could. The same way as the "absolutely needs data for the first install" can be solved in more than one way:
> ...
>
> The current way lockfile works is suitable for most configs in most situations. The edge case comes **only during first install** ...
>
> ...for which there are more than one workaround (see above) 

What is the actual workaround? A `PackChanged` handler that has specific cases for the action to take per plugin instead of the data element?

> ...for which there is a long term solution in the form of 'pkg.json' hooks.

IIRC, pkg.json hooks are for plugin maintainers. Sometimes users want to configure something specific for them, too.

> - Not doing lockfile synchronization (from which "install all plugins from the lockfile" is only one step) potentially makes lockfile and state on disk out of sync for the duration of startup. This might be problematic if the user decides to use vim.pack.get() or something during startup.

I'd still consider this too magical, but if `vim.pack.get` during startup is an important use-case, couldn't `vim.pack` only materialize this when/if `vim.pack.get` is effectively called?

But also, why is eager initialization necessary? I notice that if I manually mess around in the repos and fire up/close Neovim, nothing about the lockfile changes. See also below:

> - With the current approach there is a visible performance gain when checking if the plugin needs to be installed. Scanning the directory with installed plugins once during the first vim.pack.add() combined with lockfile sync is faster than checking if the plugin is installed on every vim.pack.add(). This can be hacked around, of course, but that would require extra complexity. Which with the previous point seems to be too much.

You could still fetch all of this information one go at the first `vim.pack.get`:

 - Construct the list of on-disk plugins.
 - Read the lockfile 

..then memoize it for future `vim.pack.add()` calls. As you say yourself, the lockfile is kept in memory and only written out when absolutely necessary.

Am I misunderstanding what "lockfile sync" actually means? Why does it need to be done eagerly?

> @justinmk  **A lot of words being spent here, which makes me think the use-case for the lockfile is not so clear cut...**

On this topic: I'm willing to entertain there is some use for a lockfile (though I personally don't need it). But that shouldn't (IMHO) lead to the observed wonkiness. If we're not willing to document "take care of the following edge cases you should take into account when PackChanged is fired, good luck", then it may be time to reconsider the trade-offs in the current approach. That doesn't necessarily mean getting rid of the lockfile, it may involve getting it to work more consistently.

> What is the actual workaround? A `PackChanged` handler that has specific cases for the action to take per plugin instead of the data element?

I've listed them in [this comment](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38139#issuecomment-4031245427). And even added one more that came to mind when writing this.

> If we're not willing to document "take care of the following edge cases you should take into account when PackChanged is fired, good luck"

It is already documented to the extent that seemed a reasonable compromise between being useful and not overly verbose:

- A [note](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/fc9058509560b4f654e008e48eb8718dd48dc8e4/runtime/doc/pack.txt#L392-L393) about install hooks and installation from the lockfile in the example of hooks.
- A [note](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/fc9058509560b4f654e008e48eb8718dd48dc8e4/runtime/doc/pack.txt#L358) that events are triggered when executing `vim.pack` methods. I *think* it should be enough to understand as they are triggered immediately, as methods themselves perform actions immediately (i.e. not scheduled).
- Per Lua basics, the fact that autocommand for `PackChanged` doesn't contain the `data` comes from it not being yet executed. I.e. the editor has no way of knowing about it yet.

---

@aktau, I've spent a lot of effort already describing how things work internally and why. Including my best effort to help fixing this case, which itself does not really fit into initial `vim.pack` design goal of "simple plugin manager; if want anything more complex - use different plugin manager".

I do have a strong opinion that the current behavior is optimal in a sense that 1) it works for the vast majority of real world configs, 2) if it doesn't work - it is a one time event per setting up fresh config on the machine, 3) there is a plan to make it even this less painful (`pkg.json` support + `vim.pack.config()` to disable lockfile).

So I am sorry, but I am not "willing to entertain" the idea of spending more hours on crafting the responses to even more questions. Especially since this now seems to have expanded into the realm of general anecdotes. If you still are not satisfied with the state of things, please create a dedicated issue with concise description of the problem which can be tracked and maybe addressed some day. Thanks.

Sure. It's your thing, after all.

On your last reply though:

> * A [note](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/fc9058509560b4f654e008e48eb8718dd48dc8e4/runtime/doc/pack.txt#L392-L393) about install hooks and installation from the lockfile in the example of hooks.

In contrast to the lines below, I agree with you it calls out some peculiarities (though I wish the peculiarities didn't exist). What it fails to mention is that in this case it will not pass the `data` element. You've mentioned before that you don't want to overdocument. I agree that it would be better to need less documentation. Anyway, so be it.

> * A [note](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/fc9058509560b4f654e008e48eb8718dd48dc8e4/runtime/doc/pack.txt#L358) that events are triggered when executing `vim.pack` methods. I _think_ it should be enough to understand as they are triggered immediately, as methods themselves perform actions immediately (i.e. not scheduled).

The doc text you reference is:

```
Performing actions via `vim.pack` functions can trigger these events:
• *PackChangedPre* - before trying to change plugin's state.
• *PackChanged* - after plugin's state has changed.
```

What I'm missing is:

 - Any `vim.pack.add` (or `vim.pack.get`) can provoke a `PackChanged`/`PackChangedPre` for any plugin (even a different one), if it is mentioned in the lockfile (or is missing from the lockfile but present on disk).

Said another way, the sub-phrase "before trying to change plugin's state" is underspecified. Which plugin?

> * Per Lua basics, the fact that autocommand for **`PackChanged` doesn't contain the `data` comes from it not being yet executed. I.e. the editor has no way of knowing about it yet.**

(Autocommands are a Vim thing, not a Lua thing. But I guess that was a typo-like.)

This highlights an important difference in understanding: I never expected `PackChanged` for plugin X to be executed **at all** given that I had not invoked `vim.pack.add({ src= "X", ... })` yet. I took me quiet a bit of print+debug.traceback debugging to figure this out.

> @aktau, I've spent a lot of effort already describing how things work internally and why. 

Yes, thanks for engaging. It seems we are not getting through to each other, so we'll have to agree to disagree.

>  ... which itself does not really fit into initial vim.pack design goal of "simple plugin manager; if want anything more complex - use different plugin manager".

My contention is that these small edge cases make it complex—not simple—for end users. Perhaps[^1] less complex to implement, but more complex to operate. Only a single person writes it, but many use it. 

I have a feeling issues with lockfile/config inconsistencies and behavioural differences based on on-disk state will rear their heads in the future once this reaches a broader audience. But I'm willing to see if I'm the only person to ever report something like this. In which case you'll be vindicated, and I'll apologize for wasting time at some point.

[^1]: Or less performant, based on your earlier statements.

repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

title: fix(pack): ensure `data` spec is passed in events during lockfile sync
